chunks.text: Balanced Search Trees Chapter 19 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Balanced Search Trees • Height of binary search tree – Sensitive to order of additions and removals • Various search trees can retain balance – Despite additions and removals © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Balanced Search Trees FIGURE 19-1 The tallest and shortest binary search trees containing the same data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If you inserted the items: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 into the tree in ascending order, you would obtain a binary search tree of maximum height If you inserted the items: 40, 20, 60, 10, 30, 50, and 70 into the tree in that order, you would obtain a binary search tree of minimum height Evolution of Self-Balancing Trees Chatgpt.com - can you please provide a chronology/evolution of the most common self-balancing trees, including 2-3 and 2-3-4 trees? Year Type Rank (Usage) Key Feature Highlights 1962 AVL Tree 5 • First self-balancing BST • Balances via height difference (balance factor ±1) • Rotation-heavy Early, rigidly balanced binary search tree, often used in compilers or memory managers 1970 2-3 Tree 3 • Multi-way search tree • Nodes can hold 1 or 2 keys (2 or 3 children) • Always height-balanced Used in theoretical data structures, academic applications, some tree-based indexers 1972 B-Trees 2 • Generalized form for databases/disks • Balanced tree for block-based storage Standard in databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite), file systems (NTFS, HFS+) 1973 Red-Black Tree 1 • Simulates 2-3-4 Trees using binary representation • Efficient and widely used Widely used in C++ STL (std::map, std::set), Java (TreeMap, TreeSet), Linux kernel 1974 2-3-4 Tree 4 • Extension of 2-3 trees with 2, 3, or 4 children • Maps directly to Red-Black Trees Extends 2-3 Tree with 4-node capability, often used in databases and file systems 1978 Splay Tree 6 • Self-adjusting, no balance info stored • Frequently accessed elements are faster Used in specialized systems (e.g., caches), but rarely in general-purpose libraries 1989 Treap 7 • Combines BST and heap using random priorities • Probabilistic balancing Used in randomized algorithms or competitive programming; rare in real-world applications 1999 AA Tree 9 • Simplified Red-Black Tree • Easier to implement but slightly less efficient Simplified variant of Red-Black Trees, used in educational contexts or hobby projects 2002 Scapegoat Trees 8 • Alternative balancing schemes • Good worst-case guarantees in certain scenarios Theoretically elegant but rarely used in production due to complex rebalancing 2002 Weight- balanced 10 • Alternative balancing schemes • Good worst-case guarantees in certain scenarios Mostly academic or in specific algorithm design contexts, rarely used in industry Binary Search Tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_search_tree • The AVL tree was invented by Georgy Adelson- Velsky and Evgenii Landis in 1962 for the efficient organization of information. It was the first self-balancing binary search tree to be invented. • Various height-balanced binary search trees were introduced to confine the tree height, such as AVL trees (1962), Treaps (1989) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treap), and red– black trees (1978) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red%E2%80%93 black_tree). AVL Trees • An AVL tree – A balanced binary search tree • Maintains its height close to the minimum • Rotations restore the balance © 2017 Pearson Education,

Velsky and Evgenii Landis in 1962 for the efficient organization of information. It was the first self-balancing binary search tree to be invented. • Various height-balanced binary search trees were introduced to confine the tree height, such as AVL trees (1962), Treaps (1989) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treap), and red– black trees (1978) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red%E2%80%93 black_tree). AVL Trees • An AVL tree – A balanced binary search tree • Maintains its height close to the minimum • Rotations restore the balance © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved AVL Trees – Balance Factor • In a binary tree the balance factor of a node X is defined to be the height difference of its two child sub-trees rooted by node X. • The subtree heights are stored at each node for all nodes in an AVL Tree, and the balance factor is calculated based on its subtree heights to check if the tree has become out of balance. • The height of a subtree is the number of edges between the root node of the subtree and the leaf node farthest down in that subtree. • Balance factor values – 0: The node is in balance. – more than 0: The node is "right heavy". – less than 0: The node is "left heavy". • If the balance factor is less than -1, or more than 1, for one or more nodes in the tree, the tree is considered not in balance, and a rotation operation is needed to restore balance. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AVL_tree https://www.w3schools.com/dsa/dsa_data_avltrees.php AVL Trees – Out-of-Balance Cases Case Description Rotation to Restore Balance Left-Left (LL) The unbalanced node and its left child node are both left-heavy. A single right rotation. Right-Right (RR) The unbalanced node and its right child node are both right-heavy. A single left rotation. Left-Right (LR) The unbalanced node is left heavy, and its left child node is right heavy. First do a left rotation on the left child node, then do a right rotation on the unbalanced node. Right-Left (RL) The unbalanced node is right heavy, and its right child node is left heavy. First do a right rotation on the right child node, then do a left rotation on the unbalanced node. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AVL_tree https://www.w3schools.com/dsa/dsa_data_avltrees.php AVL Trees – Left-Left (LL) and Right-Right (RR) AVL – add 30, 60, 70, 40, 20, and 10 30 +1 30 +1 60 0 +2 RR 30 60 +1 70 0 30 60 0 70 0 0 30 60 -1 70 0 +1 40 0 30 60 -1 70 0 0 40 0 20 0 30 60 -2 70 0 -1 40 0 20 -1 10 0 LL 30 60 0 70 0 0 40 0 20 -1 10 0 AVL Trees – Left-Right (LR) AVL – add 40, 80, 70, 30, 50, 60 40 +1 80 -1 40 0 80 -2 40 +1 70 0 LR 80 -2 40 0 70 -1 LR 80 40 0 70 0 0 80 40 -1 70 -1 30 0 80 40 0 70 -1 0 30 0 50 0 0 AVL Trees – Left-Right (LR) (cont.) AVL – add 40, 80, 70, 30, 50, 60 -2 80 40 0 70 -1 0 30 0 50 0 80 40 +1 70 -2 0 30 0 50 +1 60 0 LR LR 80 50 -1 70 0

+1 70 0 LR 80 -2 40 0 70 -1 LR 80 40 0 70 0 0 80 40 -1 70 -1 30 0 80 40 0 70 -1 0 30 0 50 0 0 AVL Trees – Left-Right (LR) (cont.) AVL – add 40, 80, 70, 30, 50, 60 -2 80 40 0 70 -1 0 30 0 50 0 80 40 +1 70 -2 0 30 0 50 +1 60 0 LR LR 80 50 -1 70 0 40 -1 60 0 30 0 50 70 0 80 0 60 0 40 -1 30 0 AVL Trees – Left-Right (RL) AVL – add 30, 70, 40, 80, 60, 50 30 +1 30 +1 70 0 30 +2 70 -1 40 0 RL 30 -2 70 0 40 -1 RL 70 30 0 40 0 0 70 30 0 40 +1 80 0 70 30 0 40 +1 0 60 0 +1 80 0 AVL Trees – Left-Right (RL) (cont.) AVL – add 30, 70, 40, 80, 60, 50 RL 60 70 +1 80 0 50 0 40 0 30 0 70 30 0 40 +1 0 60 0 80 0 70 30 0 40 +2 -1 60 -1 80 0 50 0 RL 60 30 0 40 +2 +1 50 0 70 +1 80 0 0 AVL Trees FIGURE 19-3 Correcting an imbalance in an AVL tree due to an addition by using a single rotation to the left © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 2-3 Trees https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2%E2%80%933_tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-6 A 2-3 tree of height 3 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A 2-3 tree is a tree in which each internal node (nonleaf) has either two or three children • All leaves are at the same level; the tree is height balanced • Data is kept in sorted order, with left subtree containing smaller values and right subtree containing larger values • A node with two children is called a 2-node • A node with three children is called a 3-node 2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-7 Nodes in a 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A 2-node, which has two (2) children, must contain a single data item that is > than the left child’s item and < the right child’s item • A 3-node, which has three (3) children must contain two (2) data items, S and L, that satisfy the following relationship: S is > the left child’s item(s) and < than the middle child’s item(s); L > than the middle child’s item(s) and < the right child’s item(s) • A leaf may contain either one (1) or two (2) data items. 2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-8 A 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 2-3 Trees LISTING 19-1 A header file for a class of nodes for a 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken,

the following relationship: S is > the left child’s item(s) and < than the middle child’s item(s); L > than the middle child’s item(s) and < the right child’s item(s) • A leaf may contain either one (1) or two (2) data items. 2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-8 A 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Searching a 2-3 Tree • Search of a 2-3 and shortest binary search tree approximately same efficiency – A binary search tree with n nodes cannot be shorter than log2(n + 1) – A 2-3 tree with n nodes cannot be taller than log2(n + 1) – Node in a 2-3 tree has at most two data items – Although you might visit fewer nodes when searching a 2-3 tree (vs a binary tree), you might have to make more comparisons at each node • Searching 2-3 tree is O(log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 2-3-4 Trees T is a 2-3-4 tree of height h if one of the following is true: 1. T is empty, in which case h is 0. 2. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains one data item b. TL and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. r > TL and r < TR 3. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains two data items; b. TL, TM and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. smaller r > TL and r < TM; larger r > TM and < TR 4. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains three data items; b. TL, TML, TMR and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. smallest r > TL and smallest r < TML d. middle r > TML and middle r < TMR e. largest r > TMR and largest r < TR © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved r TL TR r TL TR r TL TR TM TMR TML 2-3-4 Trees FIGURE 19-24 A 2-3-4 tree with the same data items as the 2-3 tree in Figure 19-10b © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Figure 19-10b Figure 19-24 2-3-4 Trees • Searching and traversing – Simple extensions of corresponding algorithms for a 2-3 tree • Adding data – Like addition algorithm for 2-3 tree – Splits node by moving one data item up to parent node © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Red-Black Trees • 1Invented in 1978 Leonidas Guibas and Robert Sedgewick • Self balancing binary search tree; O(log n) search/add/delete • 2Red-black tree properties – Each node is either red or black – Root node is always black in color – Maintain balance during add/remove operations – Nodes that have no child are considered the internal nodes and these nodes are connected to the NIL nodes that are always black in color. The NIL nodes are the leaf nodes in the Red-Black tree. – If the node is Red, then its children should be in Black color. – Every path from a node to any of its descendant's NIL node should have same number of black nodes. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red%E2%80%93black_tree#:~:text=AVL%20trees%20can%20be%20colored,trees%20are%20more%20rigidly%20balanced. 2https://www.javatpoint.com/red-black-tree 3https://www.eecs.umich.edu/courses/eecs380/ALG/red_black.html 3A basic red-black tree 2-3-4 vs Red-Black Trees • A 2-3-4 tree: – Balanced – Add/remove operations use one pass from root to

The NIL nodes are the leaf nodes in the Red-Black tree. – If the node is Red, then its children should be in Black color. – Every path from a node to any of its descendant's NIL node should have same number of black nodes. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red%E2%80%93black_tree#:~:text=AVL%20trees%20can%20be%20colored,trees%20are%20more%20rigidly%20balanced. 2https://www.javatpoint.com/red-black-tree 3https://www.eecs.umich.edu/courses/eecs380/ALG/red_black.html 3A basic red-black tree 2-3-4 vs Red-Black Trees • A 2-3-4 tree: – Balanced – Add/remove operations use one pass from root to leaf – Requires more storage than binary search tree • Red-black tree – Special binary tree which represents 2-3-4 tree – Advantages of a 2-3-4 tree but requires less storage • Represent each 3 node and 4 node in a 2-3-4 tree as equivalent binary search tree – All child pointers in original 2-3-4 tree are black pointers – Red pointers link 2-nodes that now contain values that were in a 3-node or a 4-node. • Worst case search, insert and delete O(log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Red-Black Trees FIGURE 19-35 Red-black representation s of a 4-node and a 3-node © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Red-Black Trees FIGURE 19-36 A red-black tree that represents the 2-3-4 tree in Figure 19-24 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Figure 19-24 2-3-4 tree Searching and Traversing a Red-Black Tree • A red-black tree is a binary search tree • Thus, search and traversal – Use algorithms for binary search tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Adding to and Removing from a Red-Black Tree • Red-black tree represents a 2-3-4 tree – Simply adjust 2-3-4 addition algorithms – Accommodate red-black representation • Splitting equivalent of a 4-node requires simple color changes – Pointer changes called rotations result in a shorter tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Red-Black vs AVL Trees © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Red-Black AVL 1, 2Height 2 * log (n + 1) 1.44 * log n 3Add/delete operations faster add/delete operations; fewer rotations are due to relatively balancing complex add/delete operations; more rotations are due to strict balancing 3Storage requires only 1 bit of information per node store balance factors or heights with each node thus requiring storage for an integer per node 3Uses used in most of the language libraries like map, multimap, multiset in C++, etc used in databases where faster retrievals are required 3Balancing Take less processing for balancing i.e.; maximum two rotation required Take more processing for balancing 1https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~siff/CS367/Notes/rbts.html#:~:text=It%20can%20be%20proven%20that,O(lg(n)). 2https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~ealexand/cs367/NOTES/AVL-Trees/index.html 3https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/red-black-tree-vs-avl-tree/ End Chapter 19 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ.
